title: Persistent Spin Texture and Spin-Orbital Hall Responses on the AgI (110) Surface
abstract: A systematic investigation of the structural, electronic, and spin-orbital transport properties of the AgI (110) surface is presented using first-principles calculations combined with analytical modelling. The non-centrosymmetric and nonsymmorphic nature of the system gives rise to a robust persistent spin texture (PST), characterized by a unidirectional spin configuration and suppressed spin relaxation, enabling an effectively infinite spin lifetime. Unlike previously reported PST materials, which are predominantly based on chalcogen compounds, this work demonstrates that a halide semiconductor can host PST, thereby significantly expanding the materials platform for spintronic applications. The underlying mechanism is captured using an effective spin-orbit coupled Hamiltonian, which reproduces the anisotropic spin splitting and momentum shift observed in the band structure. This work introduces two new analytical models describing PST and compares them with existing models, offering new perspectives on PST arising from spin-orbit interaction. In addition, the system exhibits sizable intrinsic spin Hall conductivity (SHC) and orbital Hall conductivity (OHC), highlighting its potential for efficient charge-to-spin and charge-to-orbital conversion. The PST is found to be robust against biaxial strain, structural distortion, and multilayer formation, while a vertical electric field breaks the symmetry protection and drives a transition to a Rashba-type spin texture. These findings establish AgI (110) as a promising platform for realizing long-lived spin transport and tunable spin-orbit functionalities in the low-dimensional halide systems.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the AgI (110) surface can host a robust, symmetry-protected persistent spin texture, expanding the known materials platform for spintronics from chalcogenides to halides. It shows that these spin textures are highly tunable via electric fields and strain, offering a new way to control spin and orbital transport.
main_problem: Investigating whether halide semiconductors can host symmetry-protected persistent spin textures (PST) and exploring the tunability of spin-orbital Hall responses via strain and electric fields.
main_result: The AgI (110) surface is found to host a robust PST that is resistant to strain and structural distortion, but can be transitioned to a Rashba-type texture by a vertical electric field.
method: First-principles DFT calculations using Quantum Espresso and Wannier90, combined with analytical modeling using effective spin-orbit coupled Hamiltonians and the Kubo formula.
model_or_system: The (110) surface of gamma-phase Silver Iodide (AgI), a non-centrosymmetric and nonsymmorphic zinc-blende structure, modeled via an effective Hamiltonian capturing anisotropic spin splitting.
key_observables: Spin Hall conductivity (SHC), orbital Hall conductivity (OHC), spin-projected band structure, spin-projected Fermi surface, and momentum offset (Q).
important_parameters: Bandgap of 1.85 eV, spin-orbit coupling constants (0.3-0.48 eV-Angstrom), biaxial strain (up to 4%), and vertical electric field strength.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the stability of the surface structure and relies on the accuracy of the pseudoDojo library pseudopotentials and the density functional approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows surface geometry, phonon stability, and electronic band structures; Figure 2 displays spin-projected bands and Fermi surfaces; Figure 6-8 illustrate the modulation of bands and Hall conductivities under strain and electric fields; Figure 9 details multilayer AgI properties.
paper_structure: The paper begins with structural and stability analysis, moves to the identification of the PST and its electronic properties, develops analytical models for the spin-orbit interaction, investigates the effects of external perturbations (strain and electric fields), and concludes with the study of multilayer configurations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
